content.problemStatement: Let us calculate the sum of k-th powers of natural numbers from 1 to n. As the result can be quite large, output the result modulo some integer p.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space-separated integers n, k and p.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing (1^k + 2^k + … + n^k) mod p.

solution.approach: The sum S_k(n)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} i^k is a polynomial in n of degree k+1 with rational coefficients. For a fixed k we pre‑compute y[i]=S_k(i) for i=0..k+1 using fast exponentiation (mod p). Then we evaluate the polynomial at any n using Lagrange interpolation in O(k) time. All operations are performed modulo p; modular inverses are obtained with the extended Euclidean algorithm (which works because p is prime, so every denominator is invertible unless n\le k+1, in which case we directly return y[n]). This gives O(k) per test case after O(k^2) preprocessing for each distinct k.
solution.timeComplexity: O(k) per test case after O(k^2) preprocessing for each distinct k (k \u2264 100).
